target.name: Vinylindoles (general class)
target.motivation: 2- or 3-vinylindole motifs are prevalent in pharmaceutical compounds and natural products, and serve as versatile building blocks, rendering this transformation particularly valuable. The development of methods enabling precise and tunable site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation at either the C2 or C3 position of 2,3-dialkylindoles represents a valuable scientific endeavor.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Dehydrogenation of indole derivatives (with carbonyl group at benzylic/homobenzylic position)
Dehydrogenation of indole derivatives (with carbonyl group at benzylic/homobenzylic position)
Dehydrogenation of indole derivatives (with carboxyl group at homobenzylic position)
Dehydrogenation of indole derivatives (with carboxyl group at homobenzylic position)
C3-benzylic dehydrogenative desaturation of 2,3-dialkylindole (ambiguine P total synthesis)

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve precise and tunable site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation at either the C2 or C3 position of 2,3-dialkylindoles, with broad substrate scope, scalability, and utility for downstream derivatizations.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C=C double bond formation at C2-benzylic position of alkylindoles
key_disconnections.reasoning: This strategy was hypothesized to allow for tunable site selectivity. For C2-benzylic dehydrogenation, PTAD was chosen as a dehydrogenative reagent, building upon ongoing research in TAD chemistry and its applications in indole functionalization. This would lead to 2-vinyl-3-alkylindoles.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C=C double bond formation at C3-benzylic position of alkylindoles
key_disconnections.reasoning: To achieve C3-benzylic dehydrogenation, a similar amination-elimination strategy was envisioned. Inspired by Lei group's catalytic benzylic pyrazolization of indoles using DDQ/TBN, this approach was adapted for amination followed by elimination to achieve C3-benzylic dehydrogenative desaturation, leading to 3-vinyl-2-alkylindoles.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy involves a sequential benzylic amination-elimination approach to achieve site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation of alkylindoles. This addresses the challenge of tunable site selectivity in molecules with multiple competitive benzylic sites, which was previously an unsolved problem, especially for alkylindoles with exclusively alkyl substituents. The choice of PTAD for C2-selectivity and DDQ/pyrazole for C3-selectivity allows for regiodivergent access to different vinylindoles from the same substrates.

thought.background: Dehydrogenation is a significant transformation, but achieving precise, tunable site selectivity in molecules with multiple competitive benzylic sites remains an unsolved challenge. Benzylic dehydrogenation of alkylindoles is largely underexplored, with existing methods often requiring carbonyl groups or directing groups. Indoles have lower benzylic C-H acidity compared to other N-heteroarenes, making conventional base-promoted transition-metal-catalyzed oxidative dehydrogenation difficult. The electron-rich nature of indolyl alkenes also increases susceptibility to overoxidation.
thought.gap: A method for site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation of 2,3-dialkylindoles at either the C2 or C3 position is needed.
thought.decision: To address the challenge of site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation of 2,3-dialkylindoles, a benzylic amination-elimination strategy will be developed. This strategy will aim for tunable site selectivity at either the C2 or C3 position.

evidence.text_span: Here, we developed site-selective benzylic dehydrogenations of 2,3-dialkylindoles via a benzylic amination-elimination strategy, filling the gap of tunable site selectivity of benzylic dehydrogenation (Scheme 1D).
evidence.section: INTRODUCTION

thought.background: The general strategy for C2-benzylic dehydrogenation involves using 4-phenyl-1,2,4-triazoline-3,5-dione (PTAD) as a dehydrogenative reagent. Hexahydrocyclohepta[b]indole (1a) is chosen as a model substrate.
thought.gap: Optimal reaction conditions (solvent, PTAD loading, temperature, time) for the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation need to be determined.
thought.decision: To optimize the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation, initial screening of solvents will be performed using PTAD (1.0 equiv) at room temperature for 15 min, followed by the addition of pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate (PPTS, 30 mol%) and heating at 100 °C for 2 h. The yield of 2a will be monitored by 1H NMR.

outcome.observation: Initial screening with MeCN as solvent gave 48% yield of 2a.

evidence.text_span: Initial screening of solvents was performed using PTAD (1.0 equiv) at room temperature for 15 min, followed by the addition of pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate (PPTS, 30 mol%) and heating at 100 °C for 2 h (Table 1, entries 1−9, for more details, see the Supporting Information). Among the solvents tested, ethanol (EtOH) provided the highest yield of desired product 2a (55% 1H NMR yield) (entry 9).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: Several solvents have been tested, with DMSO giving the highest yield so far (54%).
thought.gap: Find the most effective solvent for the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation.
thought.decision: Test EtOH as a solvent, as it is often a good choice for such reactions.

outcome.observation: EtOH provided the highest yield of desired product 2a (55% 1H NMR yield) among the initial solvent screening.

evidence.text_span: Among the solvents tested, ethanol (EtOH) provided the highest yield of desired product 2a (55% 1H NMR yield) (entry 9).

thought.background: EtOH was identified as the best solvent, giving 55% yield with 1.0 equiv of PTAD.
thought.gap: The yield is still moderate. The PTAD loading might be suboptimal.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, the PTAD loading will be increased to 1.5 equiv, keeping EtOH as the solvent and other conditions constant.

evidence.text_span: Further optimization focused on EtOH as the solvent. Increasing the PTAD loading to 1.5 equiv significantly improved the yield of 2a to 71% (entry 10), while using 2.0 equiv did not offer further benefit (entry 11).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: EtOH is the best solvent. PTAD loading of 1.0 equiv gave 55% yield.
thought.gap: The PTAD loading might be suboptimal.
thought.decision: Increase PTAD loading to 1.5 equiv to see if yield improves.

outcome.observation: Increasing PTAD loading to 1.5 equiv significantly improved the yield of 2a to 71%.

evidence.text_span: Increasing the PTAD loading to 1.5 equiv significantly improved the yield of 2a to 71% (entry 10), while using 2.0 equiv did not offer further benefit (entry 11).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: PTAD loading of 1.5 equiv gave 71% yield. Further increasing PTAD loading might improve the yield.
thought.gap: Determine if higher PTAD loading is beneficial.
thought.decision: Increase PTAD loading to 2.0 equiv to see if yield improves.

outcome.observation: Using 2.0 equiv of PTAD did not offer further benefit, with yield dropping slightly to 67%.

evidence.text_span: Increasing the PTAD loading to 1.5 equiv significantly improved the yield of 2a to 71% (entry 10), while using 2.0 equiv did not offer further benefit (entry 11).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: Optimal PTAD loading is 1.5 equiv (71% yield). Reaction time might be a factor.
thought.gap: Determine if prolonging the initial PTAD reaction time is beneficial.
thought.decision: Prolong the initial PTAD reaction time to 30 min, keeping PTAD at 1.5 equiv and other conditions constant.

outcome.observation: Prolonging the initial PTAD reaction time to 30 min did not offer further benefit, with yield dropping to 68%.

evidence.text_span: Prolonging the initial PTAD reaction time or extending the subsequent heating time with PPTS did not offer further benefit (entries 12−13).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: Prolonging initial PTAD reaction time was not beneficial. The subsequent heating time with PPTS might be a factor.
thought.gap: Determine if extending the subsequent heating time with PPTS is beneficial.
thought.decision: Extend the subsequent heating time with PPTS to 4 h, keeping PTAD at 1.5 equiv and initial PTAD reaction time at 15 min.

outcome.observation: Extending the subsequent heating time with PPTS to 4 h did not offer further benefit, with yield dropping to 69%.

evidence.text_span: Prolonging the initial PTAD reaction time or extending the subsequent heating time with PPTS did not offer further benefit (entries 12−13).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: Neither increasing PTAD loading beyond 1.5 equiv nor extending reaction times improved the yield. The reaction temperature during the PPTS-mediated step might be a critical factor.
thought.gap: Determine if increasing the temperature of the PPTS-mediated step is beneficial.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, the temperature of the PPTS-mediated step will be increased to 110 °C, keeping PTAD at 1.5 equiv and initial PTAD reaction time at 15 min.

evidence.text_span: Investigation of the reaction temperature during the PPTS-mediated step revealed that increasing the temperature to 110 °C significantly boosted the yield of 2a to 81% (entry 14).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: The best conditions so far are 1.5 equiv PTAD, EtOH, 15 min at rt, then PPTS at 100 °C for 2 h (71% yield).
thought.gap: The temperature of the PPTS-mediated step might be suboptimal.
thought.decision: Increase the temperature of the PPTS-mediated step to 110 °C.

outcome.observation: Increasing the temperature to 110 °C significantly boosted the yield of 2a to 81%.

evidence.text_span: Investigation of the reaction temperature during the PPTS-mediated step revealed that increasing the temperature to 110 °C significantly boosted the yield of 2a to 81% (entry 14).

thought.background: The optimized conditions (1.5 equiv PTAD, EtOH, 15 min at rt, then PPTS at 110 °C for 2 h) gave 81% yield.
thought.gap: The reaction needs to be scaled up to demonstrate practical utility.
thought.decision: Scale up the reaction from 0.1 to 0.5 mmol with an extended initial PTAD reaction time of 30 min to ensure complete conversion.

outcome.observation: Scaling up the reaction to 0.5 mmol with an extended initial PTAD reaction time of 30 min afforded the target product 2a in 79% isolated yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, we scaled up the reaction from 0.1 to 0.5 mmol with an extended reaction time A of 30 min, affording the target product 2a in 79% isolated yield (entry 15).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C2-Benzylic Dehydrogenation Reaction of Alkylindoles

thought.background: The C2-benzylic dehydrogenation strategy is established. The mechanism needs to be investigated.
thought.gap: Identify reactive intermediates and propose a detailed mechanism for the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation.
thought.decision: To identify reactive intermediates, compound 1a will be reacted with PTAD at room temperature for 30 min, and the products will be isolated and characterized. The isolated intermediate will then be subjected to PPTS-mediated elimination.

outcome.observation: The reaction majorly resulted in the formation of the C2-benzylic urazolation product 2-2b in 56% isolated yield. Intermediate 2-2bb was also observed, which was unstable and could not be isolated in a pure form.

evidence.text_span: To identify the reactive intermediates generated upon the reaction of PTAD and indoles, compound 1a was subjected to reaction with PTAD at room temperature for 30 min. Notably, this reaction majorly resulted in the formation of the C2-benzylic urazolation product 2−2b in 56% isolated yield. Additionally, intermediate 2−2bb was also observed, which was unstable and could not be isolated in a pure form.
evidence.section: Mechanism Studies

thought.background: Intermediate 2-2b was isolated from the reaction of 1a with PTAD.
thought.gap: Confirm if 2-2b is a key intermediate in the dehydrogenation pathway.
thought.decision: Subject 2-2b to PPTS-mediated elimination to see if it yields the desired product 2a.

outcome.observation: The 2-2b underwent efficient amino-elimination with PPTS, yielding the desired 2a in 70% yield. This indicates that 2-2b is a key intermediate.

evidence.text_span: The 2−2b underwent efficient amino-elimination with PPTS, yielding the desired 2a in 70% yield. These results indicated that 2−2b might be the key intermediate of the indole C2-benzylic dehydrogenative reaction (Scheme 3A).
evidence.section: Mechanism Studies

thought.background: The C2-benzylic urazolation product 2-2b is a key intermediate. However, prior studies suggested an Alder-ene reaction at the C3 position.
thought.gap: Reconcile the observed C2-amination with the expected C3-Alder-ene reaction and propose a comprehensive mechanism.
thought.decision: Propose a mechanism involving initial Alder-ene addition, followed by aromatization-driven 1,3-rearrangement of the allylic C-N bond to form the C2-benzylic amination intermediate, and subsequent E1 elimination.

evidence.text_span: However, based on prior studies, the reaction would undergo an electrophilic Alder-ene reaction at the C3 position of indole, yielding a relatively stable dearomatized adduct.10 To rationalize this event, we proposed a possible mechanism (Scheme 3B). Initial isomerization of the imine moiety in intermediate A generates an enamine structure B. Driven by rearomatization of the indole core, a 1,3-rearrangement of the allylic C−N bond occurs, affording C2-benzylic amination intermediate 2−2b. Furthermore, we postulated that this C−N bond is inherently weak, and given the urazole group’s capacity as a leaving group, heterolytic cleavage could occur. This generates a benzylic carbocation-urazole anion pair, which subsequently recombines to furnish the rearranged product.
evidence.section: Mechanism Studies

thought.background: A mechanism involving Alder-ene addition followed by 1,3-urazole migration was proposed.
thought.gap: Validate the proposed mechanism by monitoring the reaction intermediates.
thought.decision: Monitor the reactions of indoles with PTAD using 1H NMR spectroscopy. Three indoles (tetrahydrocarbazole, 1a, and 1q) will be reacted with PTAD in CDCl3 at 0 °C for 5 min, and then analyzed by 1H NMR.

outcome.observation: 1H NMR spectra revealed distinct intermediates: tetrahydrocarbazole formed dearomatized Alder-ene adduct 2-1a, 1a produced C2-benzylic amination intermediate 2-2b, and 1q yielded primarily dearomatized adduct 2-1c with a minor amount of C2-benzylic amination intermediate 2-2c. Over extended reaction times, the dearomatized adducts gradually isomerized to their respective C2-benzylic amination intermediates. Kinetic analysis showed fastest migration for 1a adduct, slower for 1q adduct, and slowest for tetrahydrocarbazole adduct.

evidence.text_span: To validate the proposed mechanism, we monitored the reactions of indoles with PTAD by using 1H NMR spectroscopy (Scheme 3C). Three indoles with different fused-ring systems, tetrahydrocarbazole, hexahydrocyclohepta[b]indole 1a, and cycloocta[b]indole 1q, were selected. Each was reacted with PTAD in CDCl3 at 0 °C for 5 min, followed by immediate 1H NMR analysis. The spectra revealed distinct intermediates: tetrahydrocarbazole formed dearomatized Alder-ene adduct 2−1a at this stage, 1a produced C2-benzylic amination intermediate 2−2b, and 1q yielded primarily dearomatized adduct 2−1c, with a minor amount of C2-benzylic amination intermediate 2−2c. Over extended reaction times, the dearomatized adducts derived from tetrahydrocarbazole and 1q gradually underwent isomerization to their respective C2-benzylic amination intermediates. Kinetic analysis based on 1H NMR experiments indicated that the 1,3- urazole migration proceeded fastest for the dearomatized adduct of the hexahydrocyclohepta[b]indole (completing within 5 min), slower for the cycloocta[b]indole-adduct, and slowest for the tetrahydrocarbazole-adduct.
evidence.section: Mechanism Studies

thought.background: The C2-benzylic dehydrogenation strategy is established. Now, focus on C3-benzylic dehydrogenation.
thought.gap: Achieve site-selective C3-benzylic dehydrogenation, which is challenging due to competing C2-reactivity. Initial attempts using PPTS as promoter might lead to acid-mediated side reactions.
thought.decision: React indole 3a with DDQ and pyrazole at room temperature, followed by PPTS-promoted reaction at 110 °C for 4 h. The yield of 4a will be monitored.

outcome.observation: The desired C3-dehydrogenation product 4a was obtained in low yield (<10% 1H NMR yield). No C2-benzylic dehydrogenation product was observed. The low yield was attributed to potential acid-mediated side reactions of the product.

evidence.text_span: Initial attempts reacting indole 3a with DDQ and pyrazole at room temperature, followed by PPTS-promoted reaction at 110 °C for 4 h, yielded the desired C3-dehydrogenation product 4a, albeit in low yield (<10% 1H NMR yield) (Table 2, entry 1). Significantly, no C2- benzylic dehydrogenation product was observed. We attributed the low yield to potential acid-mediated side reactions of the product.15
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

thought.background: PPTS-promoted elimination for C3-dehydrogenation resulted in low yield due to acid-mediated side reactions.
thought.gap: Find a suitable promoter for the elimination step that avoids acid-mediated side reactions.
thought.decision: Investigate pyrazole moiety elimination under basic conditions, specifically using DBU as the promoter.

evidence.text_span: Consequently, we investigated pyrazole moiety elimination under basic conditions. Pleasingly, using DBU as the promoter delivered 4a in 71% yield with excellent site selectivity and no detectable C2-benzylic dehydrogenative product.
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

thought.background: PPTS was ineffective for C3-dehydrogenation. DBU is a promising basic promoter.
thought.gap: Optimize the solvent for DBU-promoted C3-dehydrogenation.
thought.decision: Test DCM as a solvent with DBU as promoter, keeping other conditions constant (DDQ, pyrazole, rt for 10 min, then 110 °C for 4 h).

outcome.observation: Using DBU as promoter with DCM as solvent gave 30% yield of 4a.

evidence.text_span: Following evaluation of solvents with DBU (entries 2−6) identified 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as optimal 71% yield, entry 3), while DCM, dioxane, DMF, and DMSO gave inferior results (8−30% or trace yields).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

thought.background: DBU as promoter with DCM gave 30% yield. Other solvents need to be tested.
thought.gap: Find the optimal solvent for DBU-promoted C3-dehydrogenation.
thought.decision: Test DCE as a solvent with DBU as promoter.

outcome.observation: 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) was identified as optimal, giving 71% yield.

evidence.text_span: Following evaluation of solvents with DBU (entries 2−6) identified 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as optimal 71% yield, entry 3), while DCM, dioxane, DMF, and DMSO gave inferior results (8−30% or trace yields).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

thought.decision: Test DMF as a solvent with DBU as promoter.

outcome.observation: DMF gave trace yield.

evidence.text_span: Following evaluation of solvents with DBU (entries 2−6) identified 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as optimal 71% yield, entry 3), while DCM, dioxane, DMF, and DMSO gave inferior results (8−30% or trace yields).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

outcome.observation: Dioxane gave 8% yield.

evidence.text_span: Following evaluation of solvents with DBU (entries 2−6) identified 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as optimal 71% yield, entry 3), while DCM, dioxane, DMF, and DMSO gave inferior results (8−30% or trace yields).

thought.decision: Test DMSO as a solvent with DBU as promoter.

outcome.observation: DMSO gave trace yield.

evidence.text_span: Following evaluation of solvents with DBU (entries 2−6) identified 1,2-dichloroethane (DCE) as optimal 71% yield, entry 3), while DCM, dioxane, DMF, and DMSO gave inferior results (8−30% or trace yields).

thought.background: The optimized conditions for C3-benzylic dehydrogenation are DDQ, pyrazole, DCE, rt for 10 min, then DBU at 110 °C for 4 h, yielding 71%.
thought.gap: The reaction needs to be scaled up to demonstrate practical utility.
thought.decision: Scale up the reaction from 0.1 to 0.5 mmol using the optimized conditions.

outcome.observation: Scaling up the reaction from 0.1 to 0.5 mmol afforded the target product 4a in 80% isolated yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, we scaled up the reaction from 0.1 to 0.5 mmol, affording the target product 4a in 80% isolated yield (entry 3).
evidence.section: Development of Selective C3-Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles

thought.background: Both C2 and C3 selective dehydrogenation protocols have been developed and optimized at 0.5 mmol scale.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the scalability of the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation for practical applications.
thought.decision: Scale up the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation of 1a to the millimole level (3 mmol).

outcome.observation: The C2-benzylic dehydrogenation of 1a was successfully scaled to 3 mmol, furnishing 2a in 79% yield.

evidence.text_span: The process was successfully scaled to the millimole level, achieving selective C2-benzylic dehydrogenation to furnish the desired products 2a (79% yield) and 2d 54% yield), respectively.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

thought.background: Both C2 and C3 selective dehydrogenation protocols have been developed and optimized.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the scalability of the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation for other substrates.
thought.decision: Scale up the C2-benzylic dehydrogenation of 1d to the millimole level (3 mmol).

outcome.observation: The C2-benzylic dehydrogenation of 1d was successfully scaled to 3 mmol, furnishing 2d in 54% yield.

evidence.text_span: The process was successfully scaled to the millimole level, achieving selective C2-benzylic dehydrogenation to furnish the desired products 2a (79% yield) and 2d 54% yield), respectively.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

thought.background: Both C2 and C3 selective dehydrogenation protocols have been developed and optimized.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the scalability of the C3-benzylic dehydrogenation for practical applications.
thought.decision: Scale up the C3-benzylic dehydrogenation of indole 1i to the millimole level (3 mmol).

outcome.observation: The C3-benzylic dehydrogenation of indole 1i was successfully scaled to 3 mmol, furnishing 4l with 65% yield.

evidence.text_span: Similar to selective C3- benzylic dehydrogenation, under standard conditions, indole 1i could be converted to 4l with 65% yield in 3 mmol scale.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

thought.background: The dehydrogenated products offer versatile handles for downstream derivatization.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the synthetic utility of the C2-dehydrogenated product 2a through further derivatization.
thought.decision: Subject 2a to alkenyl sulfonylation with tert-butyl hydroperoxide and a sulfonyl hydrazine.

outcome.observation: Alkenyl sulfonylation of 2a yielded product 5 in 50% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subjecting 2a to reaction with tertbutyl hydroperoxide and a sulfonyl hydrazine enabled the alkenyl sulfonylation of 2a, yielding product 5,50%.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

thought.background: The dehydrogenated products offer versatile handles for downstream derivatization.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the synthetic utility of the C2-dehydrogenated product 2a through further derivatization, specifically cycloaddition reactions.
thought.decision: Subject the olefin moiety within 2a to an inverse electron-demand Diels-Alder reaction with a tetrazole.

outcome.observation: The olefin moiety within 2a participated in an inverse electron-demand Diels-Alder reaction with a tetrazole, affording the polycyclic product 6 in quantitative yield.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, the olefin moiety within 2a participated in an inverse electron-demand Diels−Alder reaction with a tetrazole, affording the polycyclic product 6 in qualitative yield.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

thought.background: The dehydrogenated products offer versatile handles for downstream derivatization.
thought.gap: Demonstrate the synthetic utility of other dehydrogenated products, specifically 2u, through cycloaddition reactions.
thought.decision: Subject compound 2u, serving as a diene, to reaction with PTAD, expecting an indole dearomatizing [4+2] cycloaddition.

outcome.observation: Compound 2u underwent an indole dearomatizing [4+2] cycloaddition with PTAD, furnishing the bridged bicyclic adduct 7 in 70% yield.

evidence.text_span: Moreover, compound 2u, serving as a diene, was subjected to reaction with PTAD, undergoing an indole dearomatizing [4 + 2] cycloaddition to furnish the bridged bicyclic adduct 7 in 70% yield.
evidence.section: Scale-up Synthesis and Product Transformations

validation.significance: The developed sequential benzylic amination-elimination strategy provides a novel and effective method for site-selective benzylic dehydrogenation of alkylindoles, addressing a long-standing challenge in organic synthesis. It offers tunable selectivity for C2 or C3 positions, broad substrate scope, scalability, and enables access to valuable vinylindole motifs for downstream derivatization. The mechanistic insights into the 1,3-urazole migration are also significant.

source.title: Site-Selective Benzylic Dehydrogenation of Alkylindoles.
source.authors: Zhang X, Kang X, Ren J, Wang J, Liu XJ.
